items.q: How does a catalyst speed up a reaction?
items.correct: It provides an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy.
items.misconception: A catalyst lowers the activation energy of the original reaction pathway.
items.random_wrong: A catalyst gives extra kinetic energy to the reactant particles.
items.why: Textbook diagrams often show 'Ea lowered' without making clear it's a new pathway, leading students to think catalysts modify the original pathway.

items.q: Why does increasing temperature increase the rate of reaction significantly?
items.correct: Mainly because a much larger fraction of collisions have energy at least equal to the activation energy; collision frequency increases only slightly.
items.misconception: Particles collide much more frequently at higher temperature, which is the main reason for the rate increase.
items.random_wrong: Higher temperature increases the concentration of the reactants.
items.why: Students focus on the more intuitive 'faster movement = more collisions' and overlook that the exponential effect comes from the energy distribution shifting past Ea.

items.q: In an exothermic reaction, what happens energetically with the bonds?
items.correct: Energy released forming new bonds exceeds energy absorbed breaking existing bonds.
items.misconception: Bond breaking releases energy and bond making absorbs energy.
items.random_wrong: The reactants get colder because they lose mass as energy.
items.why: Students confuse the direction: they think breaking is 'releasing' (like cracking something open releases contents), when actually breaking always requires energy input.

items.q: According to collision theory, a successful collision needs.
items.why: Correct answer: At least the activation energy. “Any contact at all” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Powdered marble chips react faster with acid than large lumps because.
items.correct: The powder has a larger surface area exposed
items.why: It's The powder has a larger surface area exposed. “The powder is more concentrated” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A reaction makes 60 cm³ of gas in 30 seconds. What is the mean rate, in cm³/s?
items.why: Exact answer: 2. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
